title: Maximum sum with nodes distance greater than two
description: Find the maximum sum of node values such that no two selected nodes are within distance â‰¤ 2 in the binary tree

examples.explanation: Start at root 1: Include 1 (sum=1). Go to left child 2: Cannot include 2 (distance=1 ≤2). Go to right child 3: Cannot include 3 (distance=1 ≤2). Max from including root=1. Exclude root: Go to left child 2 (sum=2), cannot include 3 (distance=2 ≤2). Go to right child 3 (sum=3). Max from excluding root=3. Overall maximum=3.

Root 10: Include 10 (sum=10). Cannot include any child (distance=1 ≤2) or grandchild (distance=2 ≤2). Max=10. Exclude root: Maximum from children/grandchildren (selecting non-adjacent nodes at distance >2) is at most 2 (e.g., two distant level-2 nodes). Compare: max=10.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion with Backtracking
approaches.summary: Explore all possible subsets of nodes in the binary tree using recursion and backtracking, checking at each step if the distance constraint (no two selected nodes within distance ≤ 2) is violated. This is straightforward but inefficient for large trees due to exponential growth.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n) where n is the number of nodes
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack and storage of selected nodes

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion with Backtracking for Maximum Sum with Distance Constraint
ladders.desc: Complete solution exploring all node subsets while enforcing distance ≤2 constraint using recursion and backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the maximum sum problem by systematically exploring all possible subsets of nodes in a binary tree while ensuring no two selected nodes are within distance ≤2. The intuition is to treat each node as a decision point: either include it in the current subset (if it doesn't violate constraints) or exclude it. We use depth-first recursion to traverse the tree, maintaining a set of selected nodes and checking constraints against ancestors up to two levels deep. The algorithm starts at the root and recursively explores both children, backtracking when constraints are violated or when leaf nodes are reached. At each step, we calculate the current sum and update the maximum when reaching leaf nodes. The time complexity is O(2^n) because we explore all subsets of n nodes, and space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and storing selected nodes. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete constraint checking and recursion framework. Common mistakes include forgetting to check distance constraints against all relevant ancestors or improper backtracking. Real code pattern involves a recursive function that takes current node, selected set, and current sum, with base cases at null nodes and constraint checks before inclusion.

examples.trace: Start at root 1: Include 1 (sum=1). Go to left child 2: Cannot include 2 (distance=1 ≤2). Go to right child 3: Cannot include 3 (distance=1 ≤2). Max from including root=1. Exclude root: Go to left child 2 (sum=2), cannot include 3 (distance=2 ≤2). Go to right child 3 (sum=3). Max from excluding root=3. Overall maximum=3.

Root 10: Include 10 (sum=10). Cannot include any child (distance=1 ≤2) or grandchild (distance=2 ≤2). Max=10. Exclude root: Maximum from children/grandchildren (selecting non-adjacent nodes at distance >2) is at most 2 (e.g., two distant level-2 nodes). Compare: max=10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Constraint Checking for Node Distance ≤2
ladders.desc: Helper function to validate if a node can be included given currently selected nodes and distance constraint.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a candidate node can be added to the current selected set without violating the distance constraint (no two selected nodes within distance ≤2). The intuition is that in a tree, distance between two nodes is the number of edges in the shortest path. For constraint ≤2, we need to ensure the candidate node is not within 2 edges of any already selected node. The algorithm maintains a set of selected nodes and, for the candidate, traverses up its ancestors (parent and grandparent) to check if any are selected, as nodes within distance 2 must share an ancestor relationship. Step-by-step: 1. If selected set is empty, return true. 2. Check if candidate's parent (if exists) is in selected set (distance=1). 3. Check if candidate's grandparent (if exists) is in selected set (distance=2). 4. Check if any sibling (via parent) is in selected set (distance=2 via common parent). 5. Return false if any check is true, else true. Time O(1) as checks are constant, space O(k) for selected set. This feeds into L0 by enabling valid subset exploration. Common mistakes: missing sibling checks or incorrect ancestor traversal. Real code: function canInclude(node, selected): if not selected: return True; if node.parent in selected: return False; if node.parent and node.parent.parent in selected: return False; for sibling in node.siblings: if sibling in selected: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Selected set has node 1. Candidate 2 has parent 1. Check: parent 1 is in selected → distance=1 ≤2 → violation → return False.
Selected set has node 1. Candidate 3 has parent 2, grandparent 1. Check: grandparent 1 is in selected → distance=2 ≤2 → violation → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Backtracking for Subset Exploration
ladders.desc: Central technique of recursively including/excluding nodes and backtracking on constraint violation.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using recursion to explore all possible subsets of nodes in a tree, with backtracking to undo decisions when constraints are violated. The intuition is to perform a depth-first traversal, making a decision at each node: include it in the current subset (if allowed) or exclude it, then recursively process children. Backtracking removes the node from the subset if included, allowing exploration of alternative paths. The algorithm: function explore(node, selected, currentSum): if node is null: update maxSum with currentSum; return; // Option 1: Exclude node explore(node.left, selected, currentSum); explore(node.right, selected, currentSum); // Option 2: Include node if canInclude(node, selected): selected.add(node); explore(node.left, selected, currentSum + node.val); explore(node.right, selected, currentSum + node.val); selected.remove(node); // backtrack. Time O(2^n) as it explores all subsets, space O(n) for recursion. This builds L1 by using constraint checks. Common mistakes: not backtracking properly or missing exclusion case. Real code: def explore(node, selected, sum): if not node: maxSum = max(maxSum, sum); return; explore(node.left, selected, sum); explore(node.right, selected, sum); if canInclude(node, selected): selected.add(node); explore(node.left, selected, sum + node.val); explore(node.right, selected, sum + node.val); selected.remove(node).

examples.trace: Start at 1: Exclude 1 → go to 2: Exclude 2 → null, sum=0; Include 2 → check canInclude(2, {}) → true, sum=2, null, max=2; Backtrack: Include 1 → check canInclude(1, {}) → true, sum=1, go to 2: Cannot include 2 (distance=1), exclude 2 → null, max=2. Final max=2.

Root 3: Exclude 3 → left 1: Exclude 1 → right 2: Exclude 2 → sum=0; Include 2 → sum=2; Include 1 → right 2 cannot include, sum=1; Include 3 → left 1 cannot include, right 2 cannot include, sum=3. Max=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal and Node Representation
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of traversing a binary tree and representing nodes with parent/child relationships.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing a binary tree node with value, left child, right child, and optionally parent reference for distance calculations, and traversing it using recursion. The intuition is that tree traversal (like DFS) allows us to visit each node systematically, which is essential for subset exploration. A node is typically defined as: class Node: val, left, right, parent. Traversal can be pre-order, in-order, or post-order; for this problem, pre-order (root, left, right) is natural as it processes the root before children. The algorithm: function traverse(node): if node is null: return; process(node); traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right). Time O(n) to visit all nodes, space O(h) for recursion depth. This supports L2 by providing the structure for recursion. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if cycles or missing null checks. Real code: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None; self.parent = None. def traverse(node): if not node: return; print(node.val); traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right).

examples.trace: Start at root 1: process 1; go left to 2: process 2; left of 2 is null, return; right of 2 is null, return; go right to 3: process 3; left null, right null. Order: 1,2,3.
Root 4: process 4; left 5: process 5; left 7: process 7; nulls; right 8: process 8; nulls; right 6: process 6; nulls. Order: 4,5,7,8,6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Set Manipulation for Selected Nodes
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of adding, removing, and checking membership in a set of selected nodes.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a set data structure to maintain the currently selected nodes during recursion, with operations to add a node when included, remove it when backtracking, and check if a node is in the set for constraint validation. The intuition is that a set provides O(1) average-time operations for add, remove, and membership test, which is efficient for frequent updates. The algorithm uses: selected.add(node) to include, selected.remove(node) to backtrack, and node in selected to check membership. Time O(1) per operation, space O(k) where k is number of selected nodes. This supports L3 by enabling management of the selected subset. Common mistakes: not removing nodes on backtracking or using a list with slower operations. Real code: selected = set(); selected.add(node); selected.remove(node); if node in selected: ...

examples.trace: Initial selected set is empty. Perform selected.add(5): set now contains 5. Check: 5 in selected returns True.
Start with selected={1,2}. Perform selected.remove(1): set becomes {2}. Check 3 in selected: 3 is not in {2}, returns False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Checks
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: understanding a binary tree node and handling null (empty) nodes.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces a binary tree node, which is a basic data structure with a value and references to left and right child nodes, and the importance of null checks to avoid errors. The intuition is that a binary tree is hierarchical, where each node has at most two children, and null represents the absence of a child, which is crucial for termination in recursion. A node is defined as: value (e.g., an integer), left (pointer to left child or null), right (pointer to right child or null). Null checks are used to stop recursion or traversal when there are no more nodes to process. For example, in code: if node is null: return. This prevents accessing properties of null. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a basic operation. This supports L4 by providing the node object for set operations. Common mistakes: forgetting null checks leading to null pointer errors. Real code: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. def process(node): if node is None: return; else: print(node.val).

examples.trace: Create node: val=3, left=None, right=None. Check if node is null: node is not null, so access node.val gives 3. Check left child: node.left is None, so handle as empty.
Node is None. Check: if node is None: return immediately without further operations to avoid error.
